subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: rebuilt from independent sources (UN/World Bank estimates, V-Dem, RSF, Freedom House, HRW, Amnesty, ACLED, ICG/CrisisWatch) + independent press; neither old-regime nor new-government framing used.

Pivotal context: the Assad regime fell in December 2024; a transitional government under Ahmed al-Sharaa now governs with partial control, Western sanctions easing in 2025, and reconstruction beginning - amid deep fragility and sectarian-violence episodes. Data is fragmentary and confidence is low.

Scored as strongly negative near-term with a genuine but highly uncertain recovery long-tail (near/long splits used); the asymmetric upside from the post-Assad opening and sanctions easing gives a positive outcome-distribution skew from a deeply negative base.

Recalibrated 2026-05-29 for the 2026 Iran war (Operation Epic Fury, US+Israel vs Iran from 28 Feb) and the Strait of Hormuz closure: blockade of ~1/5 of world oil+LNG, attacks/threats on Gulf desalination (water security), and the global energy shock. The two-week 8 Apr ceasefire collapsed by late May — the US renewed strikes on Iran ~25-26 May (Trump convened Camp David) and the war resumed, with Hormuz still blockaded. (Reuters; BBC; NYT; CNN; ISW; Al Jazeera; CSIS; Wikipedia)

Confidence refresh (2026-05-29): the post-Assad transition is well-documented by multilateral sources (UN Security Council, EU Council, US Congress, IMF) - sub-factor confidences raised and observability moves opaque->mixed as Syria opens to international scrutiny, though outcomes stay uncertain (positive skew retained).

economic.notes: A war-devastated economy and collapsed currency, with sanctions easing and reconstruction the uncertain upside.

fiscal_state.notes: GDP about a third of pre-war, but IDA arrears cleared and the World Bank re-engaged, with a huge reconstruction need. (World Bank)

monetary_independence.notes: A collapsed, heavily-dollarized pound and a central bank rebuilding. (Syria Direct; Al Jazeera)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: No reserve role, with minimal FX reserves. (Reuters)

inflation.notes: Very high and collapse/subsidy-removal-driven, stabilizing later. (Syrian Observer)

banking_stability.notes: A shattered system reconnecting as sanctions lift. (House of Commons Library)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: War-damaged oil and agriculture plus a captagon-trade legacy, against 2025 Gulf/Turkey investment commitments. (CRS)

institutional.notes: A transitional government building from a totalitarian collapse, with collapsed data and an improving-from-the-floor press.

rule_of_law.notes: A transitional government with no established rule of law yet and open transitional-justice questions. (UN CoI; Atlantic Council)

statistical_integrity.notes: Collapsed and largely absent - insufficient reliable data. (BTI 2026)

press_freedom.notes: Post-Assad transition has loosened the totalitarian media clamp and the country is under heavy international scrutiny (UN/EU/US engagement); still restrictive and unconsolidated. (Security Council Report; RSF)

political_social.notes: A fragile transitional government with partial control and sectarian-violence episodes.

political_stability.notes: Partial control, 2025 coastal and Druze sectarian killings, Kurdish-SDF integration talks, and Israeli incursions - deeply fragile. (HRW; ACLED; Al Jazeera)

civil_liberties.notes: The Assad-era totalitarian apparatus is dismantled under the Al-Sharaa transitional government (UN de-listed Sharaa/HTS Dec 2025; EU lifted most sanctions 2025-26); liberties remain restricted and ISIS attacks persist (1 May Damascus attack), but markedly less closed than under Assad. (UNSC; EU Council)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Refugee-producing, with early 2025 returns beginning but called tentative. (UNHCR)

social_cohesion.notes: Deeply fractured along Sunni/Alawite/Druze/Kurd lines, with war trauma and identity-based killings. (HRW; ACLED)

demographics.notes: Mass displacement and brain-drain, with returns beginning. (UNHCR; World Bank)

geopolitical.notes: A new government courting the Gulf/Turkey/West with sanctions easing, against residual conflict.

alliance_reliability.notes: Courting the Gulf, Turkey, and the West with sanctions eased, but unproven and in-flux. (CRS)

conflict_involvement.notes: Iranian-linked sites and supply lines were struck on Syrian territory during the 2026 war, compounding the post-civil-war fragility. (Wikipedia)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Decades of heavy US sanctions ended in 2025 and the World Bank re-engaged - a genuine positive shift. (House of Commons Library; CRS)

physical_practical.notes: War-destroyed infrastructure and a collapsed health system, with severe drought.

climate.notes: Severe drought (a conflict trigger), water scarcity, extreme heat, with readiness destroyed by war. (IPCC AR6; WB)

infrastructure.notes: Power, water, and housing in ruins, with a massive reconstruction need. (World Bank)

crime_safety.notes: Sectarian violence and lawless zones, improving unevenly in parts. (ACLED; HRW)

disaster_insurance.notes: A negligible market - insufficient reliable data. (general)

summary: A country at a genuine but highly uncertain inflection point: the Assad regime fell in December 2024 and a transitional government under Ahmed al-Sharaa now governs with only partial control, as Western sanctions ease and reconstruction begins. The near-term reality is dire - a war-devastated economy (GDP about a third of pre-war), a collapsed currency, shattered infrastructure and health systems, deep Sunni/Alawite/Druze/Kurd fractures with 2025 sectarian killings, and residual ISIS and Israeli-strike conflict. But the post-Assad opening and sanctions easing give a real asymmetric recovery upside from a deeply negative base. Data is fragmentary and confidence is low.
